title: Random Perturbation of Toeplitz Matrices
speaker: Charles Bordenave
abstract:
In 1947 John Von Neumann and Herman Goldstine, while developing the IAS computing machines, wrote a seminal paper on numerical errors in matrix computations. They suggested modeling the "computing noise" (coming from rounding errors, transcendental operations,...) by "probabilistic noise".  Nearly 80 years later, this pioneering idea remains an insightful paradigm in mathematical programming and numerical analysis. 
In this lecture, we will explain how this perspective of Von Neumann and Goldstine can help us to understand outrageous numerical errors made by our current computers when computing eigenvalues of badly conditioned matrices. 
We will take the example of Toeplitz matrices. These matrices form a rich class of matrices which appear in many areas of mathematics, physics and engineering. Toeplitz matrices are notoriously highly sensitive to small perturbations. They also come with a century-old rich theory that allows for a remarkably precise description of their spectral behavior under random perturbations.
The talk is based on a joint work with Mireille Capitaine and François Chapon.

proof_stages.label: Toeplitz symbols and smoothed random model
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines finite-band Toeplitz matrices through their Laurent-polynomial symbols and sets up the random perturbation model T_N(a)+sigma_N Y_N with iid noise in a chosen unitary basis, allowing sigma_N to tend to zero but not exponentially fast.

proof_stages.label: Global spectral regularization and limiting support
proof_stages.summary: Known results are recalled: circulant matrices are diagonalized by Fourier transform, the empirical eigenvalue measures of Toeplitz plus noise converge to the same deterministic beta_sigma as the noisy circulant model, and the support of beta_sigma is explicitly described by an integral inequality.

proof_stages.label: Outlier problem and winding-number dichotomy
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates the local point process of eigenvalues in a domain outside the limiting support and identifies the winding number of a-z around the domain as the conditioning invariant: zero winding gives no outliers, while nonzero winding leads to zeros of a random analytic function.

proof_stages.label: Finite-rank reduction to a stable noisy circulant
proof_stages.summary: A Toeplitz matrix is rewritten as a circulant matrix minus a finite-rank term PQ, so the problem becomes a finite-rank perturbation of the stable matrix S=C+sigma_N Y_N, whose resolvent fluctuations against fixed vectors are encoded in a renormalized matrix-valued master field.

proof_stages.label: Sylvester determinant reduction of eigenvalues
proof_stages.summary: Using Sylvester’s identity det(I+AB)=det(I+BA), zeros of the large characteristic determinant of S-PQ are reduced to zeros of the small determinant det(I-Q(S-z)^{-1}P).

proof_stages.label: Szegő asymptotics and the zero-winding regime
proof_stages.summary: The small determinant is expanded around the circulant resolvent, and the deterministic leading factor is identified as a Toeplitz/circulant determinant ratio whose nonzero limit in winding number zero follows from the strong Szegő theorem, proving absence of outliers there.

proof_stages.label: Higher adjugates and nonzero-winding zero process
proof_stages.summary: For nonzero winding, the rank of the limiting deterministic matrix has kernel dimension equal to the absolute winding number, so the first nonvanishing term in the determinant expansion is the corresponding higher-adjugate term applied to the limiting master field, giving the announced random analytic function whose zeros describe outliers.

examples.name: Hadamard/Fourier-basis numerical eigenvalues of a nilpotent Toeplitz matrix
examples.note: The speaker shows that a matrix whose true eigenvalues should be zero can numerically produce roots of unity and interior outliers, motivating the replacement of roundoff error by small random perturbations.
examples.engagement_reason: He actually runs/quotes numerical eigenvalue computations for the nilpotent Toeplitz matrix in Hadamard and Fourier bases, describing the roots-of-unity circle and outliers.

passes.reason: the underlying finite shift is nilpotent with collapsed true spectrum, and the spurious root-of-unity numerics are used to exhibit nonnormal eigensolver instability

examples.name: Wilkinson-type instability of polynomial roots
examples.note: An audience comparison with Wilkinson instability illustrates that small coefficient or computational errors can drastically move roots/eigenvalues, but it is not used in the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: Wilkinson instability is invoked as an analogy for polynomial-root ill-conditioning, with only general comments about flat characteristic polynomials and fluctuations.

passes.reason: Wilkinson-type polynomials are invoked to show that tiny coefficient perturbations can make root locations unstable

examples.name: Winding-one Gaussian case and nilpotent shift/Bergman kernel
examples.note: For winding number one with Gaussian noise, and especially for the shift symbol a(omega)=omega, the limiting random analytic function has an explicit covariance kernel, illustrating the general zero-process theorem and the rank-one determinant reduction.
examples.engagement_reason: For the winding-one Gaussian/nilpotent-shift case he specializes the limit field and explicitly gives the covariance kernel, noting the Bergman-kernel limit as noise goes to zero.

passes.reason: the example uses the nilpotent shift with collapsed spectrum, while the Gaussian/Bergman-kernel limit is a highly regular disk-invariant case
the winding-one nilpotent-shift case is the minimal nonzero-index case with a collapsed nilpotent spectrum and a highly regular Bergman-kernel limit
The explicit case uses the nilpotent one-step shift with collapsed spectrum and the canonical highly regular disk Bergman/Gaussian kernel to illustrate the limit law.
the winding-one Gaussian example uses the minimal nonzero winding case and the nilpotent shift with collapsed spectrum, yielding the highly symmetric disk Bergman kernel

examples.name: Faraday-cage analogy for fixed-vector resolvents
examples.note: The electromagnetic analogy explains why deterministic test vectors outside the spectral support do not feel the random noise to first order, motivating the name of the resolvent cancellation effect.
examples.engagement_reason: Beyond naming the Faraday-cage analogy, he formulates the fixed-vector resolvent comparison between noisy circulant and noiseless circulant matrices and explains the vanishing first-order effect and renormalized master field.

passes.reason: fixed deterministic vector resolvent probes are ordinary test objects used to explain the vanishing noise effect rather than to refute a claim
the Faraday-cage fixed-vector resolvent comparison is an analogy for noise invisibility in scalar resolvent tests, not a structurally special object or counterexample
